project_positioning: Pro-nuclear, educational, and visually engaging. The exhibit highlights nuclear energy as a clean, reliable, high-density power source that can help reduce fossil fuel use and support future electricity demand.
project_goal: Show how nuclear reactors generate electricity, why nuclear energy is valuable compared with coal, oil, and gas, and why it is increasingly relevant for a low-carbon, high-demand energy future.
core_messaging: Nuclear plants produce very low operational greenhouse gas emissions.
Nuclear fuel is extremely energy-dense compared with fossil fuels.
Nuclear power can help stabilize grids and support high electricity demand.
Nuclear is especially relevant for future loads such as AI data centers.

primary_sources.title: The Power of a Uranium Pellet Compared to Fossil Fuels
primary_sources.recommended_use: Illustrate the extraordinary energy density of uranium fuel compared with coal, oil, and gas.
primary_sources.notes: Good hero image for a pro-nuclear opening section.

primary_sources.title: What are the safest and cleanest sources of energy?
primary_sources.recommended_use: Compare deaths and lifecycle emissions across energy sources.
primary_sources.notes: Strong evidence visual for nuclear’s safety and climate advantages.

primary_sources.title: How a Nuclear Reactor Actually Works
primary_sources.recommended_use: Explain reactor heat generation, steam production, turbine rotation, and electricity production.
primary_sources.notes: Best simple animation for understanding the core process of PWR reactor. Of the nation's 94 operating reactors, roughly one-third are boiling water reactors, while the rest are pressurized water reactors. Original 2019 article URL relocated; current NEI fundamentals page used.

primary_sources.title: U.S. nuclear power plants (as of February 2025)
primary_sources.recommended_use: Show the current geographic footprint and capacity distribution of U.S. nuclear power plants in the opening scene.
primary_sources.notes: EIA map labeling major plants and plant capacities across the United States.
primary_sources.license_note: Public agency source; suitable for citation and reference.

primary_sources.title: Volume of U.S. Spent Nuclear Fuel
primary_sources.recommended_use: Illustrate the small physical volume of U.S. spent nuclear fuel in the safety and waste section.
primary_sources.notes: Generation Atomic graphic visualizing spent fuel volume as a football-field-sized footprint.

supporting_sources.title: Pressurized Water Reactors
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Authoritative explanation of how a common reactor type works.
supporting_sources.notes: Useful for accurate technical copy and exhibit captions.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; verify any graphic reuse terms.

supporting_sources.title: Unit 3: Nuclear Reactors / Energy Generation
supporting_sources.recommended_use: General educational overview of nuclear reactor basics and energy generation.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; verify any graphic reuse terms.

supporting_sources.title: The nuclear fuel cycle
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Explain uranium mining, enrichment, fuel use, and spent fuel handling.
supporting_sources.notes: Helpful for a lifecycle or fuel-cycle section.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; generally suitable for citation and reference.

supporting_sources.title: Smart, stable, reliable
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Support claims that nuclear can provide stable, low-carbon, 24/7 power and help grids integrate renewables.
supporting_sources.notes: Especially relevant for reliability and grid stability messaging.

supporting_sources.title: Nuclear Power: Clean Energy
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Support high-level claims about nuclear power’s low emissions and steady supply.
supporting_sources.notes: Good for a concise sustainability section.

supporting_sources.title: Nuclear Energy
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Generate charts for global generation trends, capacity, and carbon comparisons.

supporting_sources.title: Electric Power Monthly
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Create current charts for U.S. electricity generation by source.
supporting_sources.notes: Ideal for a live or updated data visualization panel.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public data source; suitable for attribution.

supporting_sources.title: Nuclear energy's role in powering data center growth
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Support a future-demand section focused on AI data centers and round-the-clock electricity needs.
supporting_sources.notes: Use as a current industry perspective, not as a primary technical source.

supporting_sources.title: NUCLEAR 101: How Does a Nuclear Reactor Work?
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Clear, illustrated explanation of how PWR and BWR light-water reactors produce electricity through fission.
supporting_sources.notes: Includes DOE-produced infographic diagrams of both reactor types. Good primary reference for the how-it-works section.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; suitable for citation and reference.

supporting_sources.title: What is Nuclear Energy? The Science of Nuclear Power
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Comprehensive overview covering fission, reactor operation, fuel cycle, waste management, and nuclear power's role in climate change mitigation.
supporting_sources.notes: Updated for COP30 (2025). Includes IAEA-produced graphics on fission and PWR design. Also useful as a timeline anchor since it covers 400+ global reactors and projections to 2050.

supporting_sources.title: Advanced Small Modular Reactors (SMRs)
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Introduce next-generation reactor technology: smaller footprint, flexible siting, incremental capacity additions.
supporting_sources.notes: Covers NuScale design approval, DOE R&D program, and deployment timeline (late 2020s–early 2030s). Good forward-looking content for the future-demand section.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; suitable for citation and reference.

supporting_sources.title: What was the death toll from Chernobyl and Fukushima?
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Address the two most well-known nuclear accidents with peer-reviewed, data-driven analysis to counter common misconceptions.
supporting_sources.notes: Puts accident deaths in context against routine fossil-fuel mortality. Directly supports the museum's goal of correcting safety misconceptions.

supporting_sources.title: Spent Fuel Storage
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Explain how spent nuclear fuel is safely stored and managed after use in a reactor.
supporting_sources.notes: Authoritative NRC overview of wet and dry storage methods. Helps frame waste as a managed engineering challenge rather than an unsolved problem.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; verify any graphic reuse terms.

supporting_sources.title: Electricity Mix
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Interactive charts showing each country's electricity generation by source, including nuclear share over time.
supporting_sources.notes: Useful for data-driven visuals in the hero or timeline sections showing nuclear's global contribution.

supporting_sources.title: Nuclear explained
supporting_sources.recommended_use: Top-level EIA portal for U.S. nuclear energy data: generation statistics, plant locations, fuel cycle overview, and waste information.
supporting_sources.notes: Good general-purpose reference that links to detailed sub-pages. Complements the more specific EIA sources.
supporting_sources.license_note: Public agency source; generally suitable for citation and reference.

timeline_sources.title: What is Nuclear Energy? The Science of Nuclear Power
timeline_sources.recommended_use: General historical and policy context for nuclear power development, plus projections to 2050.
timeline_sources.notes: Replaces former /topics/nuclear-power page (now 404). This IAEA educational article covers the same ground with additional detail on fission, fuel cycle, waste, and climate.

timeline_sources.title: World Energy Needs and Nuclear Power
timeline_sources.recommended_use: Historical context and future outlook for nuclear in the global energy mix.
timeline_sources.notes: Useful for milestone framing and long-term trends.

timeline_sources.title: Nuclear Energy Overview
timeline_sources.recommended_use: Build an interactive timeline of U.S. nuclear generation, capacity, and share of electricity.
timeline_sources.notes: Best source for a clean chart-based timeline.
timeline_sources.license_note: Public data source; suitable for attribution.

recommended_exhibit_sections.title: Powering AI and the Future Grid

tone_guidance: Use pro-nuclear framing, but keep the language museum-like rather than promotional.
Emphasize benefits with evidence: reliability, low carbon emissions, fuel efficiency, and grid stability.
Acknowledge safety and waste honestly, but frame them as engineering and policy challenges that can be managed.
Avoid anti-fossil-fuel rhetoric that sounds polemical; instead compare measured outcomes like emissions, deaths, and reliability.
implementation_notes: Prefer public-agency and data sources for factual claims.
Use your three existing visuals as the centerpiece of the exhibit.
For timeline visuals, generate charts from EIA and IAEA data rather than relying on static third-party images.
Always attribute images and charts in the UI.
Before publishing, verify reuse rights for all non-government assets.
